note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 34-year-old female presenting with a 4-day history of burning sensation during urination localized to the genital area. She reports the symptom has remained consistent in severity, described as moderate, and is associated with increased urinary frequency. She has not tried any treatments yet. She has a known allergy to penicillin and a history of renal disease. She is not pregnant. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
- Working diagnosis (most likely): Acute uncomplicated cystitis 
- Differential: 
 1. Urinary tract infection (UTI) 
 2. Urethritis (possibly infectious or irritant) 
 3. Vaginitis (e.g., candidiasis, bacterial vaginosis) 
 4. Interstitial cystitis or other non-infectious causes of dysuria 
- Red flags considered: No fever, flank pain, or systemic symptoms reported; no hematuria or signs of pyelonephritis mentioned.

P: 
- Disposition: Manage as outpatient with close follow-up if symptoms worsen or systemic signs develop. 
- Plan: Given the clinical presentation consistent with uncomplicated cystitis and the patient’s penicillin allergy, recommend initiating a non-penicillin antibiotic regimen appropriate for uncomplicated UTI, such as nitrofurantoin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (if no sulfa allergy), for 5 days. Patient should increase fluid intake and avoid irritants such as harsh soaps or douches. Symptomatic treatment with phenazopyridine may be considered for dysuria relief. Review renal disease history carefully before prescribing antibiotics to avoid nephrotoxic agents. 
- Safety net: Advise the patient to seek urgent care if she develops fever, flank pain, worsening symptoms, inability to urinate, or signs of systemic infection. If symptoms persist beyond 48-72 hours after starting treatment, or if new symptoms arise, she should follow up promptly.

evidence.title: Burn Evaluation: MedlinePlus Medical Test
evidence.snippet:
ep into the skin 
Is larger than 3 inches across 
Looks leathery and dry 
Has patches of white, brown, or black 
Swells very fast 
Gets more painful over time, has pus, or causes a fever , which may be signs of an infection 
Also, get medical help for all burns that:
Are caused by electricity or chemicals 
Are on the face, hands, genitals, buttocks, over a joint, or go all the way around a part of the body, such as a leg or the chest 
Happen with inhalation (breathing in) of smoke, hot gasses, or chemicals 
Happen to a child under age 5 or an adult older than age 70 
References 
Agrawal A, Raibagkar SC, Vora HJ. Friction Burns: Epidemiology and Prevention. Ann Burns Fire Disasters [Internet]. 2008 Mar 31 [cited 2023 Mar 8]; 21(1): 3-6. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3188131 
American Burn Association [Internet]. Chicago (IL): American Burn Association; c2022

Conditions A to Z
Cystitis 
Cystitis is a urinary tract infection (UTI) that affects the bladder. It's common, particularly in women. It often gets better by itself, but may sometimes be treated with antibiotics. 
Some people get cystitis frequently and may need regular or long-term treatment.
Symptoms of cystitis 
Symptoms of cystitis include:
pain, burning or stinging when you pee needing to pee more often and urgently than usual pee that's dark, cloudy or strong smelling pain low down in your tummy Symptoms in young children may also include:
a high temperature – they feel hotter than usual if you touch their neck, back or tummy wetting themselves reduced appetite and being s
